problem_summary: 2024 MCM/ICM Problem D (Great Lakes water-level management). The team had to (1) determine a monthly "optimal" water level for the five Great Lakes that balances five competing stakeholder groups, (2) build control algorithms that drive the lakes toward those levels using the only two human-controllable levers (the upstream Compensating Works/Soo Locks and the downstream Moses-Saunders Dam), (3) run sensitivity analysis on the two dams' outflows and benchmark against real 2017 levels, (4) test algorithm robustness to environmental shocks (heavy precipitation, ice jams), and (5) focus narrowly on Lake Ontario / Ottawa River / Montreal stakeholders. It is an operations-research / control problem dressed as a hydrology problem: a multi-objective optimization to set targets, then a dynamical-system + feedback-control design to reach them. Team #2417831, Outstanding award.
models_used: Genetic Algorithm (GA) for the multi-objective optimal-water-level search (Task 1) — historical monthly average used as the seed/initial individual, fitness = weighted aggregate benefit function
Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to derive the five stakeholder weights (P=0.31 Protection, E=0.27 Ecological, L=0.22 Leisure, H=0.10 Hydro-power, S=0.10 Shipping) from a pairwise-comparison matrix

Five hand-crafted benefit/cost functions, one per stakeholder: coastal-protection cost (step function epsilon(x) x shoreline length x population density), hydro-power revenue (proportional to head/altitude drop), leisure satisfaction (std-dev of monthly levels = flow stability), shipping revenue (function of sqrt of upstream-downstream level difference), ecological effect (annual max-min level spread x lake area)
Lake-chain hydrodynamic ODE model: dh_i/dt = (Q_in - Q_out)/A_i, lakes modeled as a serial chain Superior -> Michigan&Huron -> St.Clair -> Erie -> Ontario
Manning-Strickler (Manning's) open-channel flow formula to derive inter-lake outflow Q_out = N w^(8/3) sqrt(g(h_i - h_{i+1}) - f*d) for the connecting rivers
Hysteresis/time-delay parameter tau on the Huron->Erie link to capture the lake's storage lag (St. Clair treated as a river, not a chain node)

Mode-switchable feedback controllers: a P -> P+PI controller on the Compensating Works (switches to PI when downstream Huron-Michigan cumulative error exceeds threshold I_0) and a PI <-> flood-release controller on Moses-Saunders Dam (flood mode: Q_out = min{Q_max, Q_in + Q_rain - Q_ev})

Prediction module (a lightweight MPC-style receding-horizon optimizer): forecasts lake levels 200 h ahead and tunes the controller-parameter vector c = (Kp1, Ki1, I0, h_control, Kp2, Ki2) by gradient descent on J(c) = sum of squared level errors
Classical PID / Model Predictive Control (MPC) presented as the baseline control theory the custom controller is synthesized from
key_techniques: Two-stage decomposition: stage 1 (GA+AHP) SETS the targets; stage 2 (hydrodynamic sim + feedback control) REACHES them — cleanly separates 'what is optimal' from 'how to achieve it'

Controllability framing: explicitly names that there are 5 lakes to control but only 2 actuators (under-actuated system), then designs the P+PI mode-switch specifically to 'decouple' control of an upstream and a downstream lake from a single dam

Deliberate model simplification with justification: collapse Michigan+Huron into one node (equal levels per addendum), treat tiny Lake St. Clair as a river segment, drop the derivative (D) term of PID because lake storage >> dam discharge so dynamics are slow
Seeding the GA with the historical mean and arguing the optimum must lie nearby (manual control range << natural lake depth) — turns a huge search space into a local refinement

Sign-handling in the fitness function: protection/leisure/ecology are minimized so their weights are negated, then the absolute value of the aggregate benefit is taken for the GA selection probability
Pragmatic optimization scoping: when full gradient-descent tuning of all 6 controller params proved too slow, they narrowed optimization to just Kp2 and Ki2 and said so

Physics-grounded parameter estimation: friction/flow-cost params f initialized from real basin topography and channel geometry (Tables 3-4), then refined by iterative calibration against observed river flows

Scenario-stress testing with a no-dam baseline: every environmental shock (rainfall, ice jam) is simulated both with and without the controller so the controller's value is shown as a difference, not asserted
Radar charts to visualize the 5-objective trade-off (historical vs optimal vs 2017) — makes a multi-objective result legible at a glance
Seasonal, time-varying re-weighting of the AHP weights for Task 5 (e.g. raise resident-safety weight in spring snowmelt, lower shipping weight in winter ice)
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it wins on completeness, physical grounding, and a genuinely sophisticated control-theory contribution that most teams would not attempt. The standout is Task 2: rather than a toy PID, they recognized the system is under-actuated (2 dams, 5 lakes), and engineered a custom mode-switchable P+PI controller plus a receding-horizon prediction module that re-tunes gains online — that is real control engineering tied to the actual hydrology. The hydrodynamic chain is built from first principles (mass balance ODE + Manning's formula + a hysteresis term) and calibrated to real flow data, so the simulation is credible rather than hand-waved. The Task-1 GA+AHP target-setting is clean and every benefit function is physically motivated (head for hydro-power, level std-dev for leisure stability, max-min spread for ecology). Crucially they close the loop: targets feed the controller, the controller is stress-tested against rainfall and ice jams with a no-dam baseline, sensitivity is shown for each actuator, and results are benchmarked against real 2017 levels. The team is also honest about limitations and pragmatic trade-offs (narrowing optimization to 2 params). The whole arc — optimize targets, simulate physics, control to targets, stress-test, localize — directly answers all five tasks with visible quantitative evidence."
section_highlights: "Summary block uses a 'For Task N:' structure that lets a judge map every paragraph to a deliverable in seconds. Assumptions are presented as explicit Assumption/Justification pairs (Michigan=Huron level, mean-level seed). Task 1 walks stakeholder -> benefit function -> AHP weight matrix -> aggregate fitness -> monthly result, with per-lake optimal-vs-historical line charts and a radar chart. Task 2 is the technical centerpiece: it first does a 'Classical Control Algorithms Synthesis' (PID, MPC) to establish baseline theory, then a 'Controller Design' section that enumerates the three failure modes of naive PID (under-actuation, fixed-gain inflexibility, parameter sensitivity) and shows how each component of their design fixes one — a very persuasive motivate-then-solve rhetorical structure. A controller block diagram (Figure 9) ties the two dam controllers to the shared prediction module. Tasks 3-4 use paired with-dam / without-dam line charts and explain the physics of each transient (e.g. Niagara Falls' ~100 m drop making Erie->Ontario flow unidirectional and explaining the dam-sensitivity split). The IJC memo restates the data sources and modeling choices in plain prose for a non-technical reader. A clean Notation table and a (visually) explicit lake-chain schema anchor the reader early."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The benefit functions carry many free proportionality coefficients (L_i, D_i, k_i, A_i, Q_i) that are never numerically pinned down or sourced — the 'optimal' levels rest on unstated constants, so the optimization is more qualitative than it appears

AHP weight matrix shown (Figure 3) looks like a 0/1 binary dominance matrix rather than a proper Saaty 1-9 reciprocal pairwise matrix, and no consistency ratio (CR) is reported — a knowledgeable judge could question whether AHP was applied correctly

GA does very little real work: it is seeded at the historical mean and argued to stay nearby, so it functions as local refinement, not global multi-objective search; no convergence plot, population size, or generation count is given, and there is no Pareto front despite 5 objectives
The optimal monthly levels barely differ from the historical mean (often <0.5 m), which undercuts the claim of a meaningful optimization gain
Controller optimization was abandoned for 4 of 6 parameters (only Kp2, Ki2 tuned) due to gradient-descent cost — the prediction/MPC module is therefore only partially realized

Task 4 ice-jam section literally repeats the same two paragraphs of analysis verbatim for the no-dam and with-dam cases (lines 1290-1298), suggesting a copy-paste error and that the with-dam ice-jam result was not actually analyzed distinctly
Several figures have placeholder/degenerate data (e.g. Task-3 charts where all series sit at 184/175/174 with no visible change; OCR-corrupted topography figure), weakening the quantitative evidence
Manning's formula is for steady uniform open-channel flow; applying it instantaneously inside a transient ODE chain is a strong approximation that is not validated
Stakeholder set and weights are self-admittedly not comprehensive and not grounded in professional/economic data
reusable_patterns: Target-then-track decomposition: use a multi-objective optimizer (GA/AHP) to define a desired set-point, then a separate dynamical-system + feedback controller to drive the system there. Reusable for any 'set a goal and then steer toward it' OR problem.

Seed-near-the-answer trick: when manual control authority is small relative to the natural state, seed the optimizer at the historical/empirical mean and justify a local search — cuts search cost and improves credibility.

Under-actuation as a headline insight: explicitly count actuators vs controlled states; if controllers < states, design mode-switching / decoupling logic and SAY that is why naive control fails. Judges reward this kind of systems thinking.

Motivate-then-solve for the centerpiece model: list the concrete failure modes of the obvious baseline (here: fixed-gain PID's three weaknesses), then introduce each model component as the fix for one named weakness.
With-vs-without baseline for every stress test: always simulate the shock scenario both with and without your intervention so the benefit is a measured delta, not an assertion.

Physics-first benefit functions: derive each objective from a physical proportionality (head for power, sqrt level-difference for navigable flow, level std-dev for flow stability, max-min spread for ecology) instead of arbitrary scoring — makes a soft multi-objective problem defensible.
Receding-horizon gain auto-tuning: wrap a feedback controller in a short-horizon predict-then-gradient-descent loop to adapt parameters online — a lightweight, presentable alternative to full MPC.
'For Task N:' summary scaffolding + Assumption/Justification pairs + a Notation table — a structural template that makes any multi-task MCM paper instantly navigable for judges.
Seasonal/time-varying re-weighting of multi-objective weights to handle a 'focus on sub-region' or 'over time' follow-up task without rebuilding the model.
